title: WLC 103-106: The First Commandment
speaker: Elder Andrew Hoy
source_files: 06-25-23 - WLC - 103 and 106 - The 1st Commandment - Eleder Andrew Hoy.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Quiz on the First Commandment
subpoints: Quiz demonstrates that the first commandment means more than not subscribing to a false religion
The Westminster Larger Catechism is very helpful in unpacking the full scope of the first commandment
Overview of the two-week structure: this week on the text itself, next week on WLC Q104-105

outline.title: Question 1: Are There More Gods Than One?
subpoints: Only one true God: the Creator-creature distinction (Genesis 1:1)
The first commandment speaks of "other gods" — how do we account for this language?
Henotheism (many gods but Yahweh demands sole allegiance) vs. biblical monotheism
Mormonism as a contemporary form of polytheism: the eternal progression of gods
Key passages: Isaiah 43, Isaiah 44:6, Psalm 86:10, Deuteronomy 4:35
Genesis 1:1 as proof of monotheism: God exists before creation; he alone created all things

outline.title: Question 2: Are the Beings Called "Gods" in Scripture Merely Fictions?
subpoints: Paul in 1 Corinthians 8:4 affirms that idols are "nothing in the world"
Elijah and the prophets of Baal: Baal has no true power
Isaiah 44 on the foolishness of idolatry — carving a god from the same wood used for fire
Yet 1 Corinthians 10:18-20 says Gentile sacrifices are made to demons, not to nothing
Pharaoh's magicians in Exodus 7 had real (limited) power
Balaam, the Witch of Endor, and Job as examples of real demonic power
The oracle at Delphi and pagan rulers may have experienced real demonic transactions
Conclusion: pagan gods are fictions as conceived by their worshipers, but demonic realities underlie much idolatry
The temptation of idolatry heightened if there were real (if temporary) demonic benefits to be gained

outline.title: Question 3: Does "Before Me" Mean Other Gods Are Allowed If Ranked Below Yahweh?
subpoints: "Before me" means "before my face" or "in my presence" — it emphasizes the heinousness of the sin
WLC Q106 addresses this: God takes special notice of and is much displeased with having any other gods
The sin is especially heinous for covenant people in whose midst God dwells
Illustration: bringing an adulterous partner into one's own home in the presence of one's spouse (Kevin DeYoung)
Christians are held to a higher standard; knowing the true God makes idolatry more inexcusable

outline.title: Question 4: Why Is the First Commandment Framed in the Negative?
subpoints: Eight of the ten commandments are negative; only commandments four and five are positive
Negative commands are more liberating: they prohibit specific things while leaving all else free
Positive commands would narrow the field; negative commands preserve wide latitude of freedom
God's design for humanity is not restriction but blessing; sin necessitates the commands
God delights to bless his people and does not take pleasure in restricting them

outline.title: Question 5: What Is the Difference Between the First and Second Commandments?
subpoints: First commandment: prohibition of worshiping false gods
Second commandment: prohibition of worshiping the true God in a false manner
The Golden Calf incident: Israel thought they were worshiping Yahweh (Exodus 32:4-5) — Aaron declared a feast "to the LORD" (Yahweh)
Applications: praying to saints (first commandment violation) vs. vain repetitions or bargaining with God (second commandment violation)
A teaser for the second commandment — coming in a future lesson

summary:
Elder Andrew Hoy teaches on Westminster Larger Catechism questions 103 to 106, focusing primarily on the text of the first commandment — "You shall have no other gods before me" — and examining six questions that emerge from it. He uses an engaging opening quiz to demonstrate that most people in the congregation do not practice overt idolatry, but then shows that the first commandment is far more comprehensive than mere rejection of false religions.

A central section addresses whether the "gods" mentioned in Scripture are merely fictions. Drawing on 1 Corinthians 8 and 10, Elder Hoy argues that while false gods are indeed fictions as conceived by their worshipers (idols are nothing), there may be real demonic realities underlying pagan sacrifice — Paul explicitly says Gentile sacrifices are made to demons. He connects this to Old Testament examples of real but limited demonic power (Pharaoh's magicians, Balaam, the Witch of Endor), and suggests this heightens the seriousness of the temptation to idolatry: if demonic powers could offer real if temporary rewards, the sin carried a much more potent temptation than if it were merely imaginary.

The lesson also clarifies important distinctions: the phrase "before me" in the commandment does not imply other gods may be tolerated if ranked lower than Yahweh — it means "in my very presence," emphasizing the audacious heinousness of the sin. And the first and second commandments, though they appear redundant to Catholic and Lutheran tradition (which merges them), address distinct sins: worshiping false gods versus worshiping the true God falsely. The lesson closes with a preview of next week's detailed exploration of what the Larger Catechism says the first commandment requires and forbids.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer

Gracious and merciful Father, we thank you that you are a God who is near your people, a good and gracious God, worthy of all our praise. As we consider the first commandment this morning, pray that we would be convicted, and more than that, that we would delight in your word, knowing that your commands are good and life-giving. Bless this time for our edification. In Jesus' name, amen.

## Introduction

For those visiting or who have not been with us: we are well into our series on the Ten Commandments using the Westminster Larger Catechism as our guide. After several weeks of prefatory material, today we finally arrive at the first commandment — covering questions 103 to 106. My main focus this morning will be the text of the commandment itself (WLC Q103), with some comments from Q106. Next week I will dive into the large questions 104 and 105 — long lists of what the first commandment requires and forbids — with a focus on how to delight in God's law, as Psalm 1:2 calls us to do.

### A Short Quiz

Let me begin with a quiz — all yes or no, raise your hand if yes applies to you.

Do you have a household idol you bow down to each night? Do you make sacrifices to the Greek pantheon — Zeus, Poseidon, Athena? The Norse gods — Thor, Loki, Freya? Do you worship Allah, Vishnu, or Ahura Mazda? Do you practice Buddhism, Shintoism, or Sikhism? Do you subscribe to the beliefs of Jehovah's Witnesses, Mormons, or Unitarians? Do you profess atheism and live exclusively for your own desires? Does anyone worship any god other than the Triune God of the Bible?

The first commandment says, "You shall have no other gods before me." It doesn't appear that's the case among us. So — any questions? Actually, there is much more to this commandment than merely not subscribing to a false religion. There are many of us who know better but in practice treat the commandment as a simple check box. The Westminster Larger Catechism is very helpful here because it gives us a thorough exposition showing that the first commandment is all-encompassing — all sin in some sense is a violation of the first commandment.

This week: the text of the commandment through six questions. Next week: how WLC Q104-105 help us delight in God's law.

## Question 1: Are There More Gods Than One?

The answer is plainly no — there is only one true God. This is arguably the most basic of all Christian doctrines. But the commandment itself says "you shall have no other gods," which seems to imply there are other gods. How do we account for that language?

Some appeal to this language to espouse henotheism — the view that the Bible doesn't teach monotheism but rather that among many gods, Yahweh demands sole allegiance from Israel. This is mostly a liberal scholarly position, but you can find it on YouTube, and it is also very close to Mormon theology. Mormonism teaches an "eternal progression of gods" — Heavenly Father was once a human who earned his divinity, as did the God above him, and so on, eternally backward. Likewise, faithful Mormons may become gods of their own planets in the future.

Where would you go in Scripture to demonstrate that there is only one true God? The Shema of Deuteronomy 6 is one option, though some argue it speaks more of the unity of God than of his uniqueness. Isaiah 43 and 44 are very helpful. Isaiah 44:6: "Besides me there is no God." Isaiah 44 as a whole chapter is devoted to the foolishness of idolatry. Psalm 86:10: "You alone are God." Deuteronomy 4:35: "There is none other beside him."

Perhaps the simplest starting point is Genesis 1:1 — in the beginning, God. He already exists before anything else. He created the heavens and the earth — the totality of all created things. And no other being is mentioned as existing alongside him. This is what we call the creator-creature distinction: there is God, and then there is everything else. There is only one God, the Triune God of Scripture.

## Question 2: Are the Beings Called "Gods" in Scripture Merely Fictions?

We've established there is only one God. So when the first commandment speaks of "other gods," are those beings merely imaginary — fictions in people's minds?

There is a sense in which they are fictions. Paul says in 1 Corinthians 8:4: "An idol is nothing in the world and there is no other God but one." The encounter between Elijah and the prophets of Baal (1 Kings 18) demonstrates that Baal has no real power — he can't even light a fire. Isaiah 44 brilliantly mocks idolaters who take a tree, burn half of it for warmth, and carve the other half into a god.

And yet Paul in 1 Corinthians 10:18-20 says something significant: "The things which the Gentiles sacrifice, they sacrifice to demons and not to God." He doesn't say they're making sacrifice to nothing. There are real spiritual realities at work in pagan idolatry.

Consider the evidence of real demonic power in Scripture:

- Exodus 7: Pharaoh's magicians replicate some of Moses's signs — they appear to have real power, though limited and far exceeded by God's.
- Job: Satan has real power to afflict and destroy, though always under God's sovereign permission.
- Balaam: There is something about Balaam that suggests he had genuine power to curse — which is why Balak hired him. God thwarted that power when it was aimed at Israel.
- 1 Samuel 28: The Witch of Endor and the appearance of Samuel.

I think it's conceivable — if not likely — that much of ancient pagan worship involved real demonic transactions: make a sacrifice, potentially receive something from those spiritual powers in return. This doesn't mean the demonic could ultimately satisfy, and every sin gives only temporary pleasure. But it heightens the seriousness of the temptation. If there were real, if fleeting, rewards to be gained from idolatry, that's a much more potent temptation than if it were entirely imaginary.

So: the gods are fictions as conceived by their worshipers (Zeus is not a real being), but real demonic powers may have underlain much of the pagan sacrifice and worship in the ancient world.

## Question 3: Does "Before Me" Mean We Can Worship Other Gods, As Long As They Are Ranked Below Yahweh?

Some read the commandment as allowing other gods in a subordinate role. But WLC Q106 addresses this: the phrase "before me" (or "before my face" or "in my presence") emphasizes the heinousness of the sin, not a permission to have subordinate gods.

God, who sees all things, takes special notice of and is greatly displeased with the sin of having any other god. This is especially true for covenant people in whose midst God dwells. Kevin DeYoung puts it well: it is comparable to an adulterer bringing his mistress into his own home in the presence of his wife. The adultery is heinous. How much more heinous to bring that adultery into your home in your wife's presence? Likewise, for Israel to engage in any idolatry in the land where God himself dwelt among them was of surpassing heinousness.

For us as Christians: we know the true God. We have experienced his goodness. We know how fleeting sin is and where true life is found. How much more inexcusable, then, when we fall into idolatry of any kind?

## Question 4: Why Is the Commandment Framed in the Negative?

Eight of the ten commandments are framed negatively ("you shall not"), while only commandments four and five are framed positively (remember the Sabbath; honor your father and mother). Why did God frame the first commandment as a prohibition rather than a positive command like Jesus's statement in Luke 4: "You shall worship the Lord and him alone"?

One possible answer: negative commands are actually more liberating than positive commands. A prohibition says you cannot do this specific thing — but everything outside that prohibition is free game. A comprehensive positive command would potentially narrow what is permitted. God's desire for humanity is not restriction but blessing. Think of the Garden of Eden: one prohibition, and everything else is given for delight. It is only because of our sinful hearts that commands and restrictions become necessary. God does not delight in holding back good things from us; he delights to bless us — but sin makes restrictions necessary.

## Question 5: What Is the Difference Between the First and Second Commandments?

They can seem redundant: no other gods, and no carved images. The Catholic and Lutheran traditions agree they are redundant — which is why they combine them into one and split the tenth commandment into two to maintain the number ten.

But the traditional Protestant distinction is important:
- **First commandment**: prohibition of worshiping false gods.
- **Second commandment**: prohibition of worshiping the true God in a false manner.

The clearest illustration is the Golden Calf. In Exodus 32:4-5, Aaron fashions the calf and says, "This is your god, O Israel, that brought you out of the land of Egypt." They are not claiming to worship Baal — they think they are worshiping Yahweh. Aaron even declares, "Tomorrow is a feast to the LORD" — to Yahweh, the covenant God of Israel. They think the Golden Calf represents Yahweh. They are worshiping the true God falsely, not a different god entirely.

Applications for us:
- First commandment violation: praying to God in the morning and then praying to saints or other deities.
- Second commandment violation: praying to the God of the Bible but in ways not recognized in Scripture — vain repetitions, superstitious practices, bargaining or giving God ultimatums ("If you do this, I will...").

Since prayer is an act of worship, to pray falsely is to worship falsely — a violation of the second commandment.

Question 6 (how we know the commandment entails more than overt idolatry) will be our launching point for next week, when we look at WLC Q104-105 in detail.

*Closing prayer.*
